title: Hebrew language

infobox.value: [Mishnaic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew") extinct as a [spoken language](/wiki/First_language "First language") by the 5th century CE, surviving as a [liturgical language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language") along with [Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") for [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism")[[1]](#cite_note-ASB-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-e19-4)

[Revived in the late 19th century CE](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language"). 9 million speakers of [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"), of which 5 million are native speakers and 3.3 million are second language speakers (2018)[[4]](#cite_note-eth-5)

[Afro-Asiatic](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages")  * [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages")   + [West](/wiki/West_Semitic_languages "West Semitic languages")     - [Central](/wiki/Central_Semitic_languages "Central Semitic languages")       * [Northwest](/wiki/Northwest_Semitic_languages "Northwest Semitic languages")         + [Canaanite](/wiki/Canaanite_languages "Canaanite languages")           - [South](/wiki/Canaanite_languages#South_Canaan "Canaanite languages")             * **Hebrew**
[Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew")  * [Mishnaic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew")   + [Medieval Hebrew](/wiki/Medieval_Hebrew "Medieval Hebrew")

Active: * [Modern](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") * [Sephardi](/wiki/Sephardi_Hebrew "Sephardi Hebrew") * [Mizrahi](/wiki/Mizrahi_Hebrew "Mizrahi Hebrew") * [Yemenite](/wiki/Yemenite_Hebrew "Yemenite Hebrew") * [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan_Hebrew "Samaritan Hebrew") * [Italki](/wiki/Italian_Hebrew "Italian Hebrew") Extinct: * [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylonian_vocalization "Babylonian vocalization") * [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_vocalization "Palestinian vocalization") * [Tiberian](/wiki/Tiberian_Hebrew "Tiberian Hebrew")

* [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet") * [Hebrew Braille](/wiki/Hebrew_Braille "Hebrew Braille") * [Paleo-Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet") ([Archaic Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Archaic_Biblical_Hebrew "Archaic Biblical Hebrew")) * [Imperial Aramaic script](/wiki/Imperial_Aramaic_script "Imperial Aramaic script") ([Late Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Late_Biblical_Hebrew "Late Biblical Hebrew")) * [Samaritan script](/wiki/Samaritan_script "Samaritan script") ([Samaritan Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Samaritan_Pentateuch "Samaritan Pentateuch"))
[Signed Hebrew](/wiki/Signed_Hebrew "Signed Hebrew") (oral Hebrew accompanied by sign)[[5]](#cite_note-6)
[Academy of the Hebrew Language](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language "Academy of the Hebrew Language")

Variously: `heb` – [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") `hbo` – [Classical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") (liturgical) `smp` – [Samaritan Hebrew](/wiki/Samaritan_Hebrew "Samaritan Hebrew") (liturgical) `obm` – [Moabite](/wiki/Moabite_language "Moabite language") (extinct) `xdm` – [Edomite](/wiki/Edomite_language "Edomite language") (extinct)

**Hebrew**[[note 2]](#cite_note-16) is a [Northwest Semitic language](/wiki/Northwest_Semitic_language "Northwest Semitic language") within the [Afroasiatic language family](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages"). A regional dialect of the [Canaanite languages](/wiki/Canaanite_languages "Canaanite languages"), it was natively spoken by the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") and remained in regular use as a first language until after 200 CE and as the [liturgical language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") of [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (since the [Second Temple period](/wiki/Second_Temple_period "Second Temple period")) and [Samaritanism](/wiki/Samaritanism "Samaritanism").[[15]](#cite_note-17) The language was [revived as a spoken language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") in the 19th century, and is the only successful large-scale example of [linguistic revival](/wiki/Language_revitalization "Language revitalization"). It is the only Canaanite language still spoken today. It is also one of the only two Northwest Semitic languages with contemporary speakers, the other being [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"). [[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)

The earliest examples of written [Paleo-Hebrew](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet") date to the 10th century BCE.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Nearly all of the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") is written in [Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew"), with much of its present form in the dialect that scholars believe flourished around the 6th century BCE, during the time of the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"). For this reason, Hebrew has been referred to by Jews as *[Lashon Hakodesh](/wiki/Lashon_Hakodesh "Lashon Hakodesh")* (לְשׁוֹן הַקֹּדֶש, lit. 'the holy tongue' or 'the tongue [of] holiness') since ancient times. The language was not referred to by the name *Hebrew* in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), but as *Yehudit* (transl. '[Judean](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah "History of ancient Israel and Judah")') or *Səpaṯ Kəna'an* (transl. "the language of [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan")").[[1]](#cite_note-ASB-2)[[note 3]](#cite_note-21) [Mishnah Gittin 9:8](/wiki/Gittin "Gittin") refers to the language as *Ivrit*, meaning Hebrew; however, [Mishnah Megillah](/wiki/Megillah_(Talmud) "Megillah (Talmud)") refers to the language as *Ashurit*, meaning [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria"), which is derived from the name of [the alphabet used](/wiki/Ktav_Ashuri "Ktav Ashuri"), in contrast to *Ivrit*, meaning the Paleo-Hebrew alphabet.[[19]](#cite_note-22)

Hebrew ceased to be a regular spoken language sometime between 200 and 400 CE, as it declined in the aftermath of the unsuccessful [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt"), which was carried out against the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") by the Jews of [Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)").[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-OxfordDictionaryChristianChurch-24)[[note 4]](#cite_note-Sáenz-BadillosRH-26) Aramaic and, to a lesser extent, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") were already in use as international languages, especially among societal elites and immigrants.[[23]](#cite_note-27) Hebrew survived into the [medieval period](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") as the language of [Jewish liturgy](/wiki/Jewish_liturgy "Jewish liturgy"), [rabbinic literature](/wiki/Rabbinic_literature "Rabbinic literature"), intra-Jewish commerce, and [Jewish poetic literature](/wiki/Jewish_literature#Poetry "Jewish literature"). The first dated book printed in Hebrew was published by [Abraham Garton](/wiki/Abraham_Garton "Abraham Garton") in [Reggio](/wiki/Reggio_Calabria "Reggio Calabria") ([Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria"), Italy) in 1475.[[24]](#cite_note-28) With the rise of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") in the 19th century, the Hebrew language [experienced a full-scale revival](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") as a spoken and literary language. The creation of a modern version of the ancient language was led by [Eliezer Ben-Yehuda](/wiki/Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda "Eliezer Ben-Yehuda"). [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") (*Ivrit*) became the main language of the [Yishuv](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv") in [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)"), and subsequently the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the [State of Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[25]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-30)

Estimates of worldwide usage include five million speakers in 1998,[[4]](#cite_note-eth-5) and over nine million people in 2013.[[27]](#cite_note-bhol-31) After Israel, the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") has the largest Hebrew-speaking population, with approximately 220,000 fluent speakers (see [Israeli Americans](/wiki/Israeli_Americans "Israeli Americans") and [Jewish Americans](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews")).[[28]](#cite_note-2009survey-32) Pre-revival forms of Hebrew are used for prayer or study in Jewish and Samaritan communities around the world today; the latter group utilizes the [Samaritan dialect](/wiki/Samaritan_Hebrew "Samaritan Hebrew") as their liturgical tongue. As a non-[first language](/wiki/First_language "First language"), it is studied mostly by non-Israeli Jews and students in Israel, by [archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeology_of_Israel "Archaeology of Israel") and [linguists](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") specializing in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [its civilizations](/wiki/History_of_the_Middle_East "History of the Middle East"), and by theologians in [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [seminaries](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary").

## Etymology

See also: [Hebrews § Etymology](/wiki/Hebrews#Etymology "Hebrews")

The modern English word *Hebrew* is derived from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *Ebrau* via Latin, from the Ancient Greek *hebraîos* (Ἑβραῖος) and [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") *'ibrāy*, all ultimately derived from [Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") *Ivri* (עברי), one of several names for the [Israelite](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") ([Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") and [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritans "Samaritans")) people – or the [Hebrews](/wiki/Hebrews "Hebrews"). It is traditionally understood to be an adjective based on the name of [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham")'s ancestor, [Eber](/wiki/Eber "Eber"), mentioned in Genesis 10:21.[[29]](#cite_note-33) The name is believed to be based on the [Semitic root](/wiki/Semitic_root "Semitic root") *ʕ-b-r* (ע־ב־ר‎), meaning 'beyond', 'other side', 'across';[[30]](#cite_note-34) interpretations of the term *Hebrew* generally render its meaning as roughly "from the other side [of the river/desert]"—i.e., an [exonym](/wiki/Exonym_and_endonym "Exonym and endonym") for the inhabitants of the land of [Israel and Judah](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah "History of ancient Israel and Judah"), perhaps from the perspective of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [Phoenicia](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") or [Transjordan](/wiki/Transjordan_(region) "Transjordan (region)") (with the river referred to being perhaps the [Euphrates](/wiki/Euphrates "Euphrates"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") or [Litani](/wiki/Litani_River "Litani River"); or maybe the northern [Arabian Desert](/wiki/Arabian_Desert "Arabian Desert") between [Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia") and [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan")).[[31]](#cite_note-35) Compare the word *[Habiru](/wiki/Habiru "Habiru")* or cognate [Assyrian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") *ebru*, of identical meaning.[[32]](#cite_note-36)

One of the earliest references to the language's name as *Ivrit* is found in the prologue to the [Book of Sirach](/wiki/Book_of_Sirach "Book of Sirach"),[[note 5]](#cite_note-37)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] from the 2nd century BCE.[[33]](#cite_note-Xeravits-38) The Hebrew Bible does not use the term *Hebrew* in reference to the language of the Hebrew people;[[34]](#cite_note-39) its later historiography, in the [Book of Kings](/wiki/Books_of_Kings "Books of Kings"), it is referred to as *Yehudit* (יְהוּדִית, lit. '[Judahite](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah")').[[35]](#cite_note-40)

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/1_QIsa_example_of_damage_col_12-13.jpg/250px-1_QIsa_example_of_damage_col_12-13.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_QIsa_example_of_damage_col_12-13.jpg)

Portion of the [Isaiah Scroll](/wiki/Isaiah_Scroll "Isaiah Scroll"), a second-century BCE manuscript of the [Biblical](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") and one of the best-preserved of the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls")

Hebrew belongs to the [Canaanite languages](/wiki/Canaanite_languages "Canaanite languages"), a branch of the [Northwest Semitic](/wiki/Northwest_Semitic_languages "Northwest Semitic languages") family.[[36]](#cite_note-41)

Hebrew was the spoken language in the Iron Age kingdoms of [Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(Samaria) "Kingdom of Israel (Samaria)") and [Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah") during the period from about 1200 to 586 BCE.[[37]](#cite_note-42)[[38]](#cite_note-:0-43) Epigraphic evidence from this period confirms the widely accepted view that the earlier layers of biblical literature reflect the language used in these kingdoms.[[38]](#cite_note-:0-43) Furthermore, the content of Hebrew inscriptions suggests that the written texts closely mirror the spoken language of that time.[[38]](#cite_note-:0-43)

Scholars debate the degree to which Hebrew was a spoken vernacular in ancient times following the [Babylonian exile](/wiki/Babylonian_exile "Babylonian exile") when the predominant international language in the region was [Old Aramaic](/wiki/Old_Aramaic "Old Aramaic").

Hebrew was extinct as a colloquial language by [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity"), but it continued to be used as a literary language, especially in Spain, as the language of commerce between Jews of different native languages,[[39]](#cite_note-44) and as the liturgical language of Judaism, evolving various dialects of literary [Medieval Hebrew](/wiki/Medieval_Hebrew "Medieval Hebrew"), until its [revival as a spoken language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") in the late 19th century.[[40]](#cite_note-45)[[41]](#cite_note-46)

### Oldest Hebrew inscriptions

Further information: [Paleo-Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet") and [Ancient Hebrew writings](/wiki/Ancient_Hebrew_writings "Ancient Hebrew writings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Silwan-inscr.jpg/250px-Silwan-inscr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silwan-inscr.jpg)

The [Shebna Inscription](/wiki/Royal_Steward_inscription "Royal Steward inscription"), from the tomb of a royal steward found in [Siloam](/wiki/Siloam "Siloam"), dates to the 7th century BCE.

In May 2023, Scott Stripling published the finding of what he claims to be the oldest known Hebrew inscription, a [curse tablet found at Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal_curse_tablet "Mount Ebal curse tablet"), dated from around 3200 years ago. The presence of the Hebrew [name of god](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Judaism "Names of God in Judaism"), Yahweh, as three letters, *Yod-Heh-Vav* (YHV), according to the author and his team meant that the tablet is Hebrew and not Canaanite.[[42]](#cite_note-47)[[43]](#cite_note-48) However, practically all professional archeologists and epigraphers apart from Stripling's team claim that there is no text on this object.[[44]](#cite_note-49)

In July 2008, Israeli archaeologist [Yossi Garfinkel](/wiki/Yosef_Garfinkel "Yosef Garfinkel") discovered [a ceramic shard](/wiki/Khirbet_Qeiyafa_ostracon "Khirbet Qeiyafa ostracon") at [Khirbet Qeiyafa](/wiki/Khirbet_Qeiyafa "Khirbet Qeiyafa") that he claimed may be the earliest Hebrew writing yet discovered, dating from around 3,000 years ago.[[45]](#cite_note-50) [Hebrew University](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") archaeologist [Amihai Mazar](/wiki/Amihai_Mazar "Amihai Mazar") said that the inscription was "proto-Canaanite" but cautioned that "[t]he differentiation between the scripts, and between the languages themselves in that period, remains unclear", and suggested that calling the text Hebrew might be going too far.[[46]](#cite_note-51)

The [Gezer calendar](/wiki/Gezer_calendar "Gezer calendar") also dates back to the 10th century BCE at the beginning of the [Monarchic period](/wiki/Monarchic_period "Monarchic period"), the traditional time of the reign of [David](/wiki/David "David") and [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon"). Classified as [Archaic Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew"), the calendar presents a list of seasons and related agricultural activities. The Gezer calendar (named after [the city](/wiki/Gezer "Gezer") in whose proximity it was found) is written in an old Semitic script, akin to the [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet") one that, through the [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Etruscans](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization"), later became the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") of [ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"). The Gezer calendar is written without any [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"), and it does not use [consonants to imply vowels](/wiki/Mater_lectionis "Mater lectionis") even in the places in which later Hebrew spelling requires them.

Numerous older tablets have been found in the region with similar scripts written in other Semitic languages, for example, [Proto-Sinaitic](/wiki/Proto-Sinaitic_script "Proto-Sinaitic script"). It is believed that the original shapes of the script go back to [Egyptian hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs"), though the phonetic values are instead inspired by the [acrophonic](/wiki/Acrophonic "Acrophonic") principle. The common ancestor of Hebrew and Phoenician is called [Canaanite](/wiki/Canaanite_languages "Canaanite languages"), and was the first to use a Semitic alphabet distinct from that of Egyptian. One ancient document is the famous [Moabite Stone](/wiki/Mesha_Stele "Mesha Stele"), written in the Moabite dialect; the [Siloam inscription](/wiki/Siloam_inscription "Siloam inscription"), found near [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), is an early example of Hebrew. Less ancient samples of Archaic Hebrew include the [ostraca found near Lachish](/wiki/Lachish_letters "Lachish letters"), which describe events preceding the final capture of Jerusalem by [Nebuchadnezzar](/wiki/Nebuchadnezzar_II_of_Babylon "Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon") and the Babylonian captivity of 586 BCE.

### Classical Hebrew

#### Biblical Hebrew

Main article: [Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew")

In its widest sense, Biblical Hebrew refers to the spoken language of ancient Israel flourishing between c. 1000 BCE and c. 400 CE.[[47]](#cite_note-dvbrbw-52) It comprises several evolving and overlapping dialects. The phases of Classical Hebrew are often named after important literary works associated with them.

* Archaic Biblical Hebrew, also called Old Hebrew or Paleo-Hebrew, from the 10th to the 6th century BCE, corresponding to the Monarchic Period until the [Babylonian exile](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity") and represented by certain texts in the Hebrew Bible ([Tanakh](/wiki/Tanakh "Tanakh")), notably the [Song of Moses](/wiki/Song_of_Moses "Song of Moses") (Exodus 15) and the [Song of Deborah](/wiki/Song_of_Deborah "Song of Deborah") (Judges 5). It was written in the [Paleo-Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet"). A script descended from this, the [Samaritan alphabet](/wiki/Samaritan_alphabet "Samaritan alphabet"), is still used by the [Samaritans](/wiki/Samaritans "Samaritans").
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Sefer-torah-vayehi-binsoa.jpg/250px-Sefer-torah-vayehi-binsoa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sefer-torah-vayehi-binsoa.jpg)

  Hebrew script used in [writing a Torah scroll](/wiki/Ktav_Stam "Ktav Stam"). Note ornamental "[crowns](/wiki/Ktav_Stam#Serifs_(tagin) "Ktav Stam")" on tops of certain letters.

   Standard Biblical Hebrew, also called Biblical Hebrew, Early Biblical Hebrew, Classical Biblical Hebrew or Classical Hebrew (in the narrowest sense), around the 8th to 6th centuries BCE, corresponding to the late Monarchic period and the Babylonian exile. It is represented by the bulk of the Hebrew Bible that attains much of its present form around this time.
* Late Biblical Hebrew, from the 5th to the 3rd centuries BCE, corresponding to the [Persian period](/wiki/Persian_period "Persian period") and represented by certain texts in the Hebrew Bible, notably the books of Ezra and Nehemiah. Basically similar to Classical Biblical Hebrew, apart from a few foreign words adopted for mainly governmental terms, and some syntactical innovations such as the use of the particle *she-* (alternative of "asher", meaning "that, which, who"). It adopted the [Imperial Aramaic script](/wiki/Aramaic_alphabet "Aramaic alphabet") (from which the modern Hebrew script descends).
* [Israelian Hebrew](/wiki/Israelian_Hebrew "Israelian Hebrew") is a proposed northern dialect of biblical Hebrew, believed to have existed in all eras of the language, in some cases competing with late biblical Hebrew as an explanation for non-standard linguistic features of biblical texts.

#### Early post-Biblical Hebrew

* [Dead Sea Scroll](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls") Hebrew from the 3rd century BCE to the 1st century CE, corresponding to the Hellenistic and Roman Periods before the [destruction of the Temple](/wiki/Destruction_of_the_Temple "Destruction of the Temple") in Jerusalem, and represented by the Qumran Scrolls that form most (but not all) of the Dead Sea Scrolls. Commonly abbreviated as DSS Hebrew, also called Qumran Hebrew. The Imperial Aramaic script of the earlier scrolls in the 3rd century BCE evolved into the [Hebrew square script](/wiki/Hebrew_square_script "Hebrew square script") of the later scrolls in the 1st century CE, also known as *ketav Ashuri* (Assyrian script), still in use today.
* [Mishnaic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew") from the 1st to the 3rd or 4th century CE, corresponding to the Roman Period after the destruction of the Temple in Jerusalem and represented by the bulk of the [Mishnah](/wiki/Mishnah "Mishnah") and [Tosefta](/wiki/Tosefta "Tosefta") within the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud") and by the Dead Sea Scrolls, notably the [Bar Kokhba](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") letters and the [Copper Scroll](/wiki/Copper_Scroll "Copper Scroll"). Also called Tannaitic Hebrew or Early Rabbinic Hebrew.

Sometimes the above phases of spoken Classical Hebrew are simplified into "Biblical Hebrew" (including several dialects from the 10th century BCE to 2nd century BCE and extant in certain Dead Sea Scrolls) and "Mishnaic Hebrew" (including several dialects from the 3rd century BCE to the 3rd century CE and extant in certain other Dead Sea Scrolls).[[48]](#cite_note-Segal-53) However, today most Hebrew linguists classify Dead Sea Scroll Hebrew as a set of dialects evolving out of Late Biblical Hebrew and into Mishnaic Hebrew, thus including elements from both but remaining distinct from either.[[49]](#cite_note-Qimron-54)

By the start of the Byzantine Period in the 4th century CE, Classical Hebrew ceased as a regularly spoken language, roughly a century after the publication of the Mishnah, apparently declining since the aftermath of the catastrophic [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") around 135 CE.

### Displacement by Aramaic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Rashiscript.PNG/250px-Rashiscript.PNG)](/wiki/File:Rashiscript.PNG)

[Rashi script](/wiki/Rashi_script "Rashi script")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/%D0%A8%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%98%D0%B0_-_Shabbat_matchbox_holder.jpg/250px-%D0%A8%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%98%D0%B0_-_Shabbat_matchbox_holder.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A8%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D1%98%D0%B0_-_Shabbat_matchbox_holder.jpg)

A silver matchbox holder with inscription in Hebrew

In the early 6th century BCE, the [Neo-Babylonian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire "Neo-Babylonian Empire") conquered the ancient [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), destroying much of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and exiling its population far to the east in [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"). During the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"), many [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites") learned [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"), the closely related Semitic language of their captors. Thus, for a significant period, the [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") elite became influenced by Aramaic.[[50]](#cite_note-55)

After [Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Great "Cyrus the Great") conquered Babylon, he allowed the Jewish people to return from captivity.[[51]](#cite_note-56)[[52]](#cite_note-57) In time, a local version of Aramaic came to be spoken in Israel alongside Hebrew. By the beginning of the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), Aramaic was the primary colloquial language of [Samarian](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria"), [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia") and [Galileean](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") Jews, and western and intellectual Jews spoke [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but a form of so-called [Rabbinic Hebrew](/wiki/Rabbinic_Hebrew "Rabbinic Hebrew") continued to be used as a vernacular in Judea until it was displaced by Aramaic, probably in the 3rd century CE. Certain [Sadducee](/wiki/Sadducees "Sadducees"), [Pharisee](/wiki/Pharisees "Pharisees"), [Scribe](/wiki/Scribe#Judaism "Scribe"), Hermit, Zealot and Priest classes maintained an insistence on Hebrew, and all Jews maintained their identity with Hebrew songs and simple quotations from Hebrew texts.[[22]](#cite_note-ASB170-25)[[53]](#cite_note-Spolsky99-58)[[54]](#cite_note-Fernandez-59)

While there is no doubt that at a certain point, Hebrew was displaced as the everyday spoken language of most Jews, and that its chief successor in the Middle East was the closely related Aramaic language, then [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"),[[53]](#cite_note-Spolsky99-58)[[note 4]](#cite_note-Sáenz-BadillosRH-26) scholarly opinions on the exact dating of that shift have changed very much.[[21]](#cite_note-OxfordDictionaryChristianChurch-24) In the first half of the 20th century, most scholars followed [Abraham Geiger](/wiki/Abraham_Geiger "Abraham Geiger") and [Gustaf Dalman](/wiki/Gustaf_Dalman "Gustaf Dalman") in thinking that Aramaic became a spoken language in the land of Israel as early as the beginning of Israel's [Hellenistic period](/wiki/History_of_Palestine#Hellenistic_period "History of Palestine") in the 4th century BCE, and that as a corollary Hebrew ceased to function as a spoken language around the same time. [Moshe Zvi Segal](/wiki/Moshe_Zvi_Segal "Moshe Zvi Segal"), [Joseph Klausner](/wiki/Joseph_Klausner "Joseph Klausner") and Ben Yehuda are notable exceptions to this view. During the latter half of the 20th century, accumulating archaeological evidence and especially linguistic analysis of the Dead Sea Scrolls has disproven that view. The Dead Sea Scrolls, uncovered in 1946–1948 near [Qumran](/wiki/Qumran "Qumran") revealed ancient Jewish texts overwhelmingly in Hebrew, not Aramaic.

The Qumran scrolls indicate that Hebrew texts were readily understandable to the average Jew, and that the language had evolved since Biblical times as spoken languages do.[[note 6]](#cite_note-61) Recent scholarship recognizes that reports of Jews speaking in Aramaic indicate a multilingual society, not necessarily the primary language spoken. Alongside Aramaic, Hebrew co-existed within Israel as a spoken language.[[56]](#cite_note-Judaism460-62) Most scholars now date the demise of Hebrew as a spoken language to the end of the [Roman period](/wiki/Judea_(Roman_province) "Judea (Roman province)"), or about 200 CE.[[57]](#cite_note-63) It continued on as a literary language down through the [Byzantine period](/wiki/History_of_Palestine#Byzantine_period "History of Palestine") from the 4th century CE.

The exact roles of Aramaic and Hebrew remain hotly debated. A trilingual scenario has been proposed for the land of Israel. Hebrew functioned as the local [mother tongue](/wiki/Mother_tongue "Mother tongue") with powerful ties to Israel's history, origins and golden age and as the language of Israel's religion; Aramaic functioned as the international language with the rest of the Middle East; and eventually Greek functioned as another international language with the eastern areas of the Roman Empire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [William Schniedewind](/wiki/William_Schniedewind "William Schniedewind") argues that after waning in the Persian period, the religious importance of Hebrew grew in the Hellenistic and Roman periods, and cites epigraphical evidence that Hebrew survived as a vernacular language – though both its grammar and its writing system had been substantially influenced by Aramaic.[[58]](#cite_note-Schniedewind-64) According to another summary, Greek was the language of government, Hebrew the language of prayer, study and religious texts, and Aramaic was the language of legal contracts and trade.[[59]](#cite_note-spolsky85-65) There was also a geographic pattern: according to [Bernard Spolsky](/wiki/Bernard_Spolsky "Bernard Spolsky"), by the beginning of the Common Era, "[Judeo-Aramaic](/wiki/Judeo-Aramaic "Judeo-Aramaic") was mainly used in Galilee in the north, Greek was concentrated in the former colonies and around governmental centers, and Hebrew monolingualism continued mainly in the southern villages of Judea."[[53]](#cite_note-Spolsky99-58) In other words, "in terms of dialect geography, at the time of the [tannaim](/wiki/Tannaim "Tannaim") Palestine could be divided into the Aramaic-speaking regions of Galilee and Samaria and a smaller area, Judaea, in which [Rabbinic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew") was used among the descendants of returning exiles."[[22]](#cite_note-ASB170-25)[[54]](#cite_note-Fernandez-59) In addition, it has been surmised that [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") was the primary vehicle of communication in coastal cities and among the upper class of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), while Aramaic was prevalent in the lower class of Jerusalem, but not in the surrounding countryside.[[59]](#cite_note-spolsky85-65) After the suppression of the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") in the 2nd century CE, Judaeans were forced to disperse. Many relocated to Galilee, so most remaining native speakers of Hebrew at that last stage would have been found in the north.[[60]](#cite_note-spolsky85b-66)

Many scholars have pointed out that Hebrew continued to be used alongside Aramaic during Second Temple times, not only for religious purposes but also for nationalistic reasons, especially during revolts such as the [Maccabean Revolt](/wiki/Maccabean_Revolt "Maccabean Revolt") (167–160 BCE) and the emergence of the [Hasmonean kingdom](/wiki/Hasmonean_kingdom "Hasmonean kingdom"), the [Great Jewish Revolt](/wiki/Great_Jewish_Revolt "Great Jewish Revolt") (66–73 CE), and the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") (132–135 CE).[[61]](#cite_note-:02-67) The nationalist significance of Hebrew manifested in various ways throughout this period. Michael Owen Wise notes that "Beginning with the time of the Hasmonean revolt [...] Hebrew came to the fore in an expression akin to modern nationalism. A form of classical Hebrew was now a more significant written language than Aramaic within Judaea."[[62]](#cite_note-68) This nationalist aspect was further emphasized during periods of conflict, as Hannah Cotton observing in her analysis of legal documents during the Jewish revolts against Rome that "Hebrew became the symbol of Jewish nationalism, of the independent Jewish State."[[63]](#cite_note-69) The nationalist use of Hebrew is evidenced in several historical documents and artefacts, including the composition of 1 Maccabees in archaizing Hebrew, [Hasmonean coinage](/wiki/Hasmonean_coinage "Hasmonean coinage") under [John Hyrcanus](/wiki/John_Hyrcanus "John Hyrcanus") (134-104 BCE), and coins from both the Great Revolt and Bar Kokhba Revolt featuring exclusively Hebrew and Palaeo-Hebrew script inscriptions.[[64]](#cite_note-:1-70) This deliberate use of Hebrew and Paleo-Hebrew script in official contexts, despite limited literacy, served as a symbol of Jewish nationalism and political independence.[[64]](#cite_note-:1-70)

The Christian [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") contains some Semitic place names and quotes.[[65]](#cite_note-71) The language of such Semitic glosses (and in general the language spoken by Jews in scenes from the New Testament) is often referred to as "Hebrew" in the text,[[66]](#cite_note-72) although this term is often re-interpreted as referring to Aramaic instead[[note 7]](#cite_note-73)[[note 8]](#cite_note-75) and is rendered accordingly in recent translations.[[68]](#cite_note-76) Nonetheless, these glosses can be interpreted as Hebrew as well.[[69]](#cite_note-77)

### Mishnah and Talmud

Main article: [Mishnaic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew")

The term "Mishnaic Hebrew" generally refers to the Hebrew dialects found in the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud"), excepting quotations from the Hebrew Bible. The dialects organize into Mishnaic Hebrew (also called [Tannaitic](/wiki/Tannaim "Tannaim") Hebrew, Early Rabbinic Hebrew, or [Mishnaic](/wiki/Mishnah "Mishnah") Hebrew I), which was a [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language"), and [Amoraic](/wiki/Amoraim "Amoraim") Hebrew (also called Late Rabbinic Hebrew or Mishnaic Hebrew II), which was a [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language"). The earlier section of the Talmud is the Mishnah that was published around 200 CE, although many of the stories take place much earlier, and were written in the earlier Mishnaic dialect. The dialect is also found in certain Dead Sea Scrolls. Mishnaic Hebrew is considered to be one of the dialects of Classical Hebrew that functioned as a living language in the land of Israel. A transitional form of the language occurs in the other works of Tannaitic literature dating from the century beginning with the completion of the Mishnah. These include the [halachic](/wiki/Midrash_halakha "Midrash halakha") [Midrashim](/wiki/Midrash "Midrash") ([Sifra](/wiki/Sifra "Sifra"), [Sifre](/wiki/Sifre "Sifre"), [Mekhilta](/wiki/Mekhilta_of_Rabbi_Ishmael "Mekhilta of Rabbi Ishmael") etc.) and the expanded collection of Mishnah-related material known as the [Tosefta](/wiki/Tosefta "Tosefta"). The Talmud contains excerpts from these works, as well as further Tannaitic material not attested elsewhere; the generic term for these passages is *[Baraitot](/wiki/Baraita "Baraita")*. The dialect of all these works is very similar to Mishnaic Hebrew.

About a century after the publication of the Mishnah, Mishnaic Hebrew fell into disuse as a spoken language. By the third century CE, sages could no longer identify the Hebrew names of many plants mentioned in the Mishnah. Only a few sages, primarily in the southern regions, retained the ability to speak the language and attempted to promote its use.[[70]](#cite_note-:3-78) According to the [Jerusalem Talmud](/wiki/Jerusalem_Talmud "Jerusalem Talmud"), Megillah 1:9: "Rebbi Jonathan from [Bet Guvrrin](/wiki/Beit_Guvrin-Maresha_National_Park "Beit Guvrin-Maresha National Park") said, four languages are appropriate that the world should use them, and they are these: The Foreign Language (Greek) for song, Latin for war, Syriac for elegies, Hebrew for speech. Some are saying, also Assyrian (Hebrew script) for writing."[[71]](#cite_note-79)[[70]](#cite_note-:3-78)

The later section of the Talmud, the [Gemara](/wiki/Gemara "Gemara"), generally comments on the Mishnah and Baraitot in two forms of Aramaic. Nevertheless, Hebrew survived as a liturgical and literary language in the form of later Amoraic Hebrew, which occasionally appears in the text of the Gemara, particularly in the Jerusalem Talmud and the classical [aggadah midrashes](/wiki/Aggadah "Aggadah").

Hebrew was always regarded as the language of Israel's religion, history and national pride, and after it faded as a spoken language, it continued to be used as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* among scholars and Jews traveling in foreign countries.[[72]](#cite_note-80) After the 2nd century CE when the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") exiled most of the Jewish population of Jerusalem following the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt"), they adapted to the societies in which they found themselves, yet letters, contracts, commerce, science, philosophy, medicine, poetry and laws continued to be written mostly in Hebrew, which adapted by borrowing and inventing terms.

### Medieval Hebrew

Main article: [Medieval Hebrew](/wiki/Medieval_Hebrew "Medieval Hebrew")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Aleppo_Codex_Joshua_1_1.jpg/250px-Aleppo_Codex_Joshua_1_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aleppo_Codex_Joshua_1_1.jpg)

[Aleppo Codex](/wiki/Aleppo_Codex "Aleppo Codex"): 10th century [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") with [Masoretic](/wiki/Masoretes "Masoretes") pointing (Joshua 1:1).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Cochin_Jewish_Inscription.JPG/250px-Cochin_Jewish_Inscription.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cochin_Jewish_Inscription.JPG)

[Kochangadi Synagogue](/wiki/Kochangadi_Synagogue "Kochangadi Synagogue") in [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"), India, dated to 1344

After the Talmud, various regional literary dialects of [Medieval Hebrew](/wiki/Medieval_Hebrew "Medieval Hebrew") evolved. The most important is [Tiberian Hebrew](/wiki/Tiberian_Hebrew "Tiberian Hebrew") or Masoretic Hebrew, a local dialect of [Tiberias](/wiki/Tiberias "Tiberias") in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") that became the standard for vocalizing the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") and thus still influences all other regional dialects of Hebrew. This Tiberian Hebrew from the 7th to 10th century CE is sometimes called "Biblical Hebrew" because it is used to pronounce the Hebrew Bible; however, properly it should be distinguished from the historical Biblical Hebrew of the 6th century BCE, whose original pronunciation must be reconstructed. Tiberian Hebrew incorporates the scholarship of the [Masoretes](/wiki/Masoretes "Masoretes") (from *masoret* meaning "tradition"), who added [vowel points](/wiki/Niqqud "Niqqud") and [grammar points](/wiki/Hebrew_cantillation "Hebrew cantillation") to the Hebrew letters to preserve much earlier features of Hebrew, for use in chanting the Hebrew Bible. The Masoretes inherited a biblical text whose letters were considered too sacred to be altered, so their markings were in the form of pointing in and around the letters. The [Syriac alphabet](/wiki/Syriac_alphabet "Syriac alphabet"), precursor to the [Arabic alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet"), also developed vowel pointing systems around this time. The [Aleppo Codex](/wiki/Aleppo_Codex "Aleppo Codex"), a Hebrew Bible with the Masoretic pointing, was written in the 10th century, likely in Tiberias, and survives into the present day. It is perhaps the most important Hebrew manuscript in existence.

During the [Golden age of Jewish culture in Spain](/wiki/Golden_age_of_Jewish_culture_in_Spain "Golden age of Jewish culture in Spain"), important work was done by grammarians in explaining the grammar and vocabulary of Biblical Hebrew; much of this was based on the work of the [grammarians](/wiki/Islamic_grammatical_tradition "Islamic grammatical tradition") of [Classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic"). Important Hebrew grammarians were [Judah ben David Hayyuj](/wiki/Judah_ben_David_Hayyuj "Judah ben David Hayyuj"), [Jonah ibn Janah](/wiki/Jonah_ibn_Janah "Jonah ibn Janah"), [Abraham ibn Ezra](/wiki/Abraham_ibn_Ezra "Abraham ibn Ezra")[[73]](#cite_note-81) and later (in [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence")), [David Kimhi](/wiki/David_Kimhi "David Kimhi"). A great deal of poetry was written, by poets such as [Dunash ben Labrat](/wiki/Dunash_ben_Labrat "Dunash ben Labrat"), [Solomon ibn Gabirol](/wiki/Solomon_ibn_Gabirol "Solomon ibn Gabirol"), [Judah ha-Levi](/wiki/Judah_ha-Levi "Judah ha-Levi"), [Moses ibn Ezra](/wiki/Moses_ibn_Ezra "Moses ibn Ezra") and [Abraham ibn Ezra](/wiki/Abraham_ibn_Ezra "Abraham ibn Ezra"), in a "purified" Hebrew based on the work of these grammarians, and in Arabic quantitative or strophic meters. This literary Hebrew was later used by Italian Jewish poets.[[74]](#cite_note-82)

The need to express scientific and philosophical concepts from [Classical Greek](/wiki/Classical_Greek_language "Classical Greek language") and [Medieval Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") motivated Medieval Hebrew to borrow terminology and grammar from these other languages, or to coin equivalent terms from existing Hebrew roots, giving rise to a distinct style of philosophical Hebrew. This is used in the translations made by the [Ibn Tibbon](/wiki/Ibn_Tibbon "Ibn Tibbon") family. (Original Jewish philosophical works were usually written in Arabic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]) Another important influence was [Maimonides](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides"), who developed a simple style based on [Mishnaic Hebrew](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew") for use in his law code, the [Mishneh Torah](/wiki/Mishneh_Torah "Mishneh Torah"). Subsequent rabbinic literature is written in a blend between this style and the Aramaized Rabbinic Hebrew of the Talmud.

Hebrew persevered through the ages as the main language for written purposes by all Jewish communities around the world for a large range of uses—not only liturgy, but also poetry, philosophy, science and medicine, commerce, daily correspondence and contracts. There have been many deviations from this generalization such as [Bar Kokhba](/wiki/Simon_bar_Kokhba "Simon bar Kokhba")'s letters to his lieutenants, which were mostly in Aramaic,[[75]](#cite_note-83) and Maimonides' writings, which were mostly in Arabic;[[76]](#cite_note-84) but overall, Hebrew did not cease to be used for such purposes. For example, the first Middle East printing press, in Safed (modern Israel), produced a small number of books in Hebrew in 1577, which were then sold to the nearby Jewish world.[[77]](#cite_note-85) This meant not only that well-educated Jews in all parts of the world could correspond in a [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible") language, and that books and legal documents published or written in any part of the world could be read by Jews in all other parts, but that an educated Jew could travel and converse with Jews in distant places, just as priests and other educated Christians could converse in Latin. For example, Rabbi [Avraham Danzig](/wiki/Avraham_Danzig "Avraham Danzig") wrote the **[Chayei Adam](/wiki/Chayei_Adam "Chayei Adam")** in Hebrew, as opposed to [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"), as a guide to *[Halacha](/wiki/Halacha "Halacha")* for the "*average* 17-year-old" (Ibid. Introduction 1). Similarly, Rabbi [Yisrael Meir Kagan](/wiki/Yisrael_Meir_Kagan "Yisrael Meir Kagan")'s purpose in writing the **[Mishnah Berurah](/wiki/Mishnah_Berurah "Mishnah Berurah")** was to "produce a work that could be studied daily so that Jews might know the proper procedures to follow minute by minute". The work was nevertheless written in Talmudic Hebrew and Aramaic, since, "the ordinary Jew [of Eastern Europe] of a century ago, was fluent enough in this idiom to be able to follow the Mishna Berurah without any trouble."[[78]](#cite_note-86)When [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") was established in the 1630s, Hebrew was a mandatory subject for all students.[[79]](#cite_note-87)

### Revival

Main article: [Revival of the Hebrew language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_at_his_desk_in_Jerusalem_-_c1912.jpg/250px-Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_at_his_desk_in_Jerusalem_-_c1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda_at_his_desk_in_Jerusalem_-_c1912.jpg)

Eliezer Ben-Yehuda

Hebrew has been [revived](/wiki/Language_revitalization "Language revitalization") several times as a literary language, most significantly by the [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah") (Enlightenment) movement of early and mid-19th-century Germany. In the early 19th century, a form of spoken Hebrew had emerged in the markets of Jerusalem between Jews of different linguistic backgrounds to communicate for commercial purposes. This Hebrew dialect was to a certain extent a [pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin").[[80]](#cite_note-88) Near the end of that century the Jewish activist [Eliezer Ben-Yehuda](/wiki/Eliezer_Ben-Yehuda "Eliezer Ben-Yehuda"), owing to the ideology of the [national revival](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") (שיבת ציון, *Shivat Tziyon*, later [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism")), began reviving Hebrew as a modern spoken language. Eventually, as a result of the local movement he created, but more significantly as a result of the new groups of immigrants known under the name of the [Second Aliyah](/wiki/Second_Aliyah "Second Aliyah"), it replaced a score of languages spoken by Jews at that time. Those languages were Jewish dialects of local languages, including [Judaeo-Spanish](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") (also called "Judezmo" and "Ladino"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), [Judeo-Arabic](/wiki/Judeo-Arabic_languages "Judeo-Arabic languages") and [Bukhori](/wiki/Bukhori_dialect "Bukhori dialect") (Tajiki), or local languages spoken in the [Jewish diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_diaspora "Jewish diaspora") such as [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language").

The major result of the literary work of the Hebrew intellectuals along the 19th century was a lexical modernization of Hebrew. New words and expressions were adapted as [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") from the large corpus of Hebrew writings since the Hebrew Bible, or borrowed from Arabic (mainly by Ben-Yehuda) and older Aramaic and Latin. Many new words were either borrowed from or coined after European languages, especially English, Russian, German, and French. Modern Hebrew became an official language in British-ruled Palestine in 1921 (along with English and Arabic), and then in 1948 became an official language of the newly declared [State of Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). Hebrew is the most widely spoken language in Israel today.

In the Modern Period, from the 19th century onward, the literary Hebrew tradition revived as the spoken language of modern Israel, called variously *Israeli Hebrew*, *Modern Israeli Hebrew*, *Modern Hebrew*, *New Hebrew*, *Israeli Standard Hebrew*, *Standard Hebrew* and so on. Israeli Hebrew exhibits some features of [Sephardic Hebrew](/wiki/Sephardic_Hebrew "Sephardic Hebrew") from its local Jerusalemite tradition but adapts it with numerous neologisms, borrowed terms (often technical) from European languages and adopted terms (often colloquial) from Arabic.

The literary and narrative use of Hebrew was revived beginning with the Haskalah movement. The first secular periodical in Hebrew, *[Ha-Me'assef](/wiki/Ha-Me%27assef "Ha-Me'assef")* (The Gatherer), was published by [maskilim](/wiki/Maskil "Maskil") in [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") (today's [Kaliningrad](/wiki/Kaliningrad "Kaliningrad")) from 1783 onwards.[[81]](#cite_note-89) In the mid-19th century, publications of several Eastern European Hebrew-language newspapers (e.g. *[Hamagid](/wiki/Hamagid "Hamagid")*, founded in [Ełk](/wiki/E%C5%82k "Ełk") in 1856) multiplied. Prominent poets were [Hayim Nahman Bialik](/wiki/Hayim_Nahman_Bialik "Hayim Nahman Bialik") and [Shaul Tchernichovsky](/wiki/Shaul_Tchernichovsky "Shaul Tchernichovsky"); there were also novels written in the language.

The [revival of the Hebrew language](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") as a [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language") was initiated in the late 19th century by the efforts of Ben-Yehuda. He joined the [Jewish national movement](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and in 1881 immigrated to [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)"), then a part of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Motivated by the surrounding ideals of renovation and rejection of the diaspora "[shtetl](/wiki/Shtetl "Shtetl")" lifestyle, Ben-Yehuda set out to develop tools for making the [literary](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language") and [liturgical language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") into everyday [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language"). However, his brand of Hebrew followed norms that had been replaced in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") by different grammar and style, in the writings of people like [Ahad Ha'am](/wiki/Ahad_Ha%27am "Ahad Ha'am") and others. His organizational efforts and involvement with the establishment of schools and the writing of textbooks pushed the [vernacularization](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") activity into a gradually accepted movement. It was not, however, until the 1904–1914 Second Aliyah that Hebrew had caught real momentum in Ottoman Palestine with the more highly organized enterprises set forth by the new group of immigrants. When the [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") recognized Hebrew as one of the country's three official languages (English, Arabic, and Hebrew, in 1922), its new formal status contributed to its diffusion. A constructed modern language with a truly Semitic vocabulary and written appearance, although often European in [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"), was to take its place among the current languages of the nations.

While many saw his work as fanciful or even [blasphemous](/wiki/Blasphemy "Blasphemy")[[82]](#cite_note-90) (because Hebrew was the holy language of the Torah and therefore some thought that it should not be used to discuss everyday matters), many soon understood the need for a common language amongst Jews of the British Mandate who at the turn of the 20th century were arriving in large numbers from diverse countries and speaking different languages. A Committee of the Hebrew Language was established. After the establishment of Israel, it became the [Academy of the Hebrew Language](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language "Academy of the Hebrew Language"). The results of Ben-Yehuda's lexicographical work were published in a dictionary (*The Complete Dictionary of Ancient and Modern Hebrew*, [Ben-Yehuda Dictionary](/wiki/Ben-Yehuda_Dictionary "Ben-Yehuda Dictionary")). The seeds of Ben-Yehuda's work fell on fertile ground, and by the beginning of the 20th century, Hebrew was well on its way to becoming the main language of the Jewish population of both Ottoman and British Palestine. At the time, members of the [Old Yishuv](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv") and a very few [Hasidic](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") sects, most notably those under the auspices of [Satmar](/wiki/Satmar_(Hasidic_dynasty) "Satmar (Hasidic dynasty)"), refused to speak Hebrew and spoke only Yiddish.

In the Soviet Union, the use of Hebrew, along with other Jewish cultural and religious activities, was suppressed. Soviet authorities considered the use of Hebrew "reactionary" since it was associated with Zionism, and the teaching of Hebrew at primary and secondary schools was officially banned by the [People's Commissariat for Education](/wiki/People%27s_Commissariat_for_Education "People's Commissariat for Education") as early as 1919, as part of an overall agenda aiming to [secularize](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") education (the language itself did not cease to be studied at universities for historical and linguistic purposes[[83]](#cite_note-91)). The official ordinance stated that Yiddish, being the spoken language of the Russian Jews, should be treated as their only national language, while Hebrew was to be treated as a foreign language.[[84]](#cite_note-92) Hebrew books and periodicals ceased to be published and were seized from the libraries, although liturgical texts were still published until the 1930s. Despite numerous protests,[[85]](#cite_note-93) a policy of suppression of the teaching of Hebrew operated from the 1930s on. Later in the 1980s in the [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), Hebrew studies reappeared due to people struggling for permission to go to Israel ([refuseniks](/wiki/Refusenik "Refusenik")). Several of the teachers were imprisoned, e.g. [Yosef Begun](/wiki/Iosif_Begun "Iosif Begun"), [Ephraim Kholmyansky](/wiki/Ephraim_Kholmyansky "Ephraim Kholmyansky"), [Yevgeny Korostyshevsky](/w/index.php?title=Yevgeny_Korostyshevsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yevgeny Korostyshevsky (page does not exist)") and others responsible for a Hebrew learning network connecting many cities of the USSR.

### Modern Hebrew

Main article: [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Annava165.jpg/500px-Annava165.jpg)](/wiki/File:Annava165.jpg)

Hebrew, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and English multilingual signs on an Israeli highway

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Hebkeyboard.JPG/250px-Hebkeyboard.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hebkeyboard.JPG)

Dual language [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_keyboard "Hebrew keyboard") and English keyboard

Standard Hebrew, as developed by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda, was based on [Mishnaic](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew") spelling and [Sephardi Hebrew](/wiki/Sephardi_Hebrew "Sephardi Hebrew") pronunciation. However, the earliest speakers of Modern Hebrew had Yiddish as their native language and often introduced [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") from Yiddish and [phono-semantic matchings](/wiki/Phono-semantic_matching "Phono-semantic matching") of international words.

Despite using Sephardic Hebrew pronunciation as its primary basis, modern Israeli Hebrew has adapted to [Ashkenazi Hebrew](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Hebrew "Ashkenazi Hebrew") [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") in some respects, mainly the following:

* the replacement of [pharyngeal articulation](/wiki/Pharyngeal_consonant "Pharyngeal consonant") in the letters *chet* ([ח](/wiki/%D7%97 "ח")‎) and *ayin* ( [ע](/wiki/%D7%A2 "ע")‎) by most Hebrew speakers with uvular [[χ](/wiki/%CE%A7 "Χ")‎] and glottal [[ʔ](/wiki/%CA%94 "ʔ")‎], respectively, by most Hebrew speakers.
* the conversion of ([ר‎](/wiki/Resh "Resh")) /r/ from an [alveolar flap](/wiki/Alveolar_flap "Alveolar flap") [ɾ] to a [voiced uvular fricative](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative") [ʁ] or [uvular trill](/wiki/Uvular_trill "Uvular trill") [ʀ], by most of the speakers, like in most varieties of standard German or Yiddish. *see [Guttural R](/wiki/Guttural_R "Guttural R")*
* the pronunciation (by many speakers) of *[tzere](/wiki/Zeire "Zeire")* < ֵ ‎> as [eɪ] in some contexts (*sifréj* and *téjša* instead of Sephardic *sifré* and *tésha*)
* the partial elimination of vocal *[Shva](/wiki/Shva "Shva")* < ְ ‎> (*zmán* instead of Sephardic *zĕman*)[[86]](#cite_note-94)
* in popular speech, penultimate stress in proper names (*Dvóra* instead of *Dĕvorá*; *Yehúda* instead of *Yĕhudá*) and some other words[[87]](#cite_note-95)
* similarly in popular speech, penultimate stress in verb forms with a second person plural suffix (*katávtem* "you wrote" instead of *kĕtavtém*).[[note 9]](#cite_note-96)

The vocabulary of Israeli Hebrew is much larger than that of earlier periods. According to [Ghil'ad Zuckermann](/wiki/Ghil%27ad_Zuckermann "Ghil'ad Zuckermann"):

> The number of attested Biblical Hebrew words is 8198, of which some 2000 are [hapax legomena](/wiki/Hapax_legomenon "Hapax legomenon") (the number of Biblical Hebrew roots, on which many of these words are based, is 2099). The number of attested Rabbinic Hebrew words is less than 20,000, of which (i) 7879 are Rabbinic par excellence, i.e. they did not appear in the Old Testament (the number of new Rabbinic Hebrew roots is 805); (ii) around 6000 are a subset of Biblical Hebrew; and (iii) several thousand are Aramaic words which can have a Hebrew form. Medieval Hebrew added 6421 words to (Modern) Hebrew. The approximate number of new lexical items in Israeli is 17,000 (cf. 14,762 in Even-Shoshan 1970 [...]). With the inclusion of foreign and technical terms [...], the total number of Israeli words, including words of biblical, rabbinic and medieval descent, is more than 60,000.[[88]](#cite_note-Zuckermann-97): 64–65

In Israel, Modern Hebrew is currently taught in institutions called [Ulpanim](/wiki/Ulpan "Ulpan") (singular: Ulpan). There are government-owned, as well as private, Ulpanim offering online courses and face-to-face programs.

## Current status

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language.JPG/250px-Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language.JPG)](/wiki/File:Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language.JPG)

[Academy of the Hebrew Language](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language "Academy of the Hebrew Language")

Modern Hebrew is the primary official language of the State of Israel. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hebrew_language&action=edit), there are about 9 million Hebrew speakers worldwide,[[89]](#cite_note-israel-hayom-hebrew-speakers-98) of whom 7 million speak it fluently.[[90]](#cite_note-esl.fis.edu-99)[[91]](#cite_note-ucl.ac.uk-100)[[92]](#cite_note-studentsabroad-101)

Currently, 90% of Israeli Jews are proficient in Hebrew, and 70% are highly proficient.[[93]](#cite_note-CBS-102) Some 60% of Israeli Arabs are also proficient in Hebrew,[[93]](#cite_note-CBS-102) and 30% report having a higher proficiency in Hebrew than in Arabic.[[27]](#cite_note-bhol-31) In total, about 53% of the Israeli population speaks Hebrew as a native language,[[94]](#cite_note-103) while most of the rest speak it fluently. In 2013 Hebrew was the native language of 49% of Israelis over the age of 20, with [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language") and [Ladino](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") being the native tongues of most of the rest. Some 26% of [immigrants from the former Soviet Union](/wiki/1990s_Post-Soviet_aliyah "1990s Post-Soviet aliyah") and 12% of Arabs reported speaking Hebrew poorly or not at all.[[93]](#cite_note-CBS-102)[[95]](#cite_note-104)

Steps have been taken to keep Hebrew the primary language of use, and to prevent large-scale incorporation of English words into the Hebrew vocabulary. The [Academy of the Hebrew Language](/wiki/Academy_of_the_Hebrew_Language "Academy of the Hebrew Language") of the [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") currently invents about 2,000 new Hebrew words each year for modern words by finding an original Hebrew word that captures the meaning, as an alternative to incorporating more English words into Hebrew vocabulary. The [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") municipality has banned officials from using English words in official documents, and is fighting to stop businesses from using only English signs to market their services.[[96]](#cite_note-105) In 2012, a [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") bill for the preservation of the Hebrew language was proposed, which includes the stipulation that all signage in Israel must first and foremost be in Hebrew, as with all speeches by Israeli officials abroad. The bill's author, MK [Akram Hasson](/wiki/Akram_Hasson "Akram Hasson"), stated that the bill was proposed as a response to Hebrew "losing its prestige" and children incorporating more English words into their vocabulary.[[97]](#cite_note-106)

Hebrew is one of several languages for which the constitution of South Africa calls to be respected in their use for religious purposes.[[8]](#cite_note-auto-9) Also, Hebrew is an official national minority language in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), since 6 January 2005.[[7]](#cite_note-auto1-8) [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") has made Hebrew a compulsory language taught in schools in the Gaza Strip.[[98]](#cite_note-107)

## Phonology

Further information: [Biblical Hebrew § Phonology](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew#Phonology "Biblical Hebrew"), and [Modern Hebrew phonology](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew_phonology "Modern Hebrew phonology")

[Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") had a typical Semitic consonant inventory, with pharyngeal /[ʕ](/wiki/Voiced_pharyngeal_fricative "Voiced pharyngeal fricative") [ħ](/wiki/Voiceless_pharyngeal_fricative "Voiceless pharyngeal fricative")/, a series of "emphatic" consonants (possibly [ejective](/wiki/Ejective_consonant "Ejective consonant"), but this is debated), lateral fricative /[ɬ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_lateral_fricative "Voiceless alveolar lateral fricative")/, and in its older stages also uvular /[χ](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative") [ʁ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative")/. /χ ʁ/ merged into /ħ ʕ/ in later Biblical Hebrew, and /[b](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_plosive "Voiced bilabial plosive") [ɡ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_plosive "Voiced velar plosive") [d](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_plosive "Voiced alveolar plosive") [k](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_plosive "Voiceless velar plosive") [p](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive") [t](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive")/ underwent allophonic spirantization to [[v](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_fricative "Voiced labiodental fricative") [ɣ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_fricative "Voiced velar fricative") [ð](/wiki/Voiced_dental_fricative "Voiced dental fricative") [x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative") [f](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative") [θ](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_fricative "Voiceless dental fricative")] (known as [begadkefat](/wiki/Begadkefat "Begadkefat")). The earliest Biblical Hebrew vowel system contained the Proto-Semitic vowels /[a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") aː [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") iː [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") uː/ as well as /[oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel")/, but this system changed dramatically over time.

By the time of the Dead Sea Scrolls, /[ɬ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_lateral_fricative "Voiceless alveolar lateral fricative")/ had shifted to /[s](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_fricative "Voiceless alveolar fricative")/ in the Jewish traditions, though for the Samaritans it merged with /[ʃ](/wiki/Voiceless_postalveolar_fricative "Voiceless postalveolar fricative")/ instead.[[49]](#cite_note-Qimron-54) The Tiberian reading tradition of the Middle Ages had the vowel system /[a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") [ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel") [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") [ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel") [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") ă ɔ̆ ɛ̆/, though other Medieval reading traditions had fewer vowels.

A number of reading traditions have been preserved in liturgical use. In Oriental ([Sephardi](/wiki/Sephardi_Hebrew "Sephardi Hebrew") and [Mizrahi](/wiki/Mizrahi_Hebrew "Mizrahi Hebrew")) Jewish reading traditions, the emphatic consonants are realized as pharyngealized, while the [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Hebrew "Ashkenazi Hebrew") (northern and eastern European) traditions have lost emphatics and pharyngeals (although according to Ashkenazi law, pharyngeal articulation is preferred over uvular or glottal articulation when representing the community in religious service such as prayer and [Torah reading](/wiki/Torah_reading "Torah reading")), and show the shift of /[w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")/ to /[v](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_fricative "Voiced labiodental fricative")/. The [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan_Hebrew "Samaritan Hebrew") tradition has a complex vowel system that does not correspond closely to the [Tiberian](/wiki/Tiberian_vocalization "Tiberian vocalization") systems.

Modern Hebrew pronunciation developed from a mixture of the different Jewish reading traditions, generally tending towards simplification. In line with [Sephardi Hebrew](/wiki/Sephardi_Hebrew "Sephardi Hebrew") pronunciation, emphatic consonants have shifted to their ordinary counterparts, /w/ to /v/, and [ɣ ð θ] are not present. Most Israelis today also merge /ʕ ħ/ with /[ʔ](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop") [χ](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative")/, do not have contrastive gemination, and pronounce /[r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")/ with a uvular pronunciation [[ʁ̞](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_approximant "Voiced uvular approximant")] rather than an alveolar one, because of Ashkenazi Hebrew influences. The consonants /[t͡ʃ](/wiki/Voiceless_postalveolar_affricate "Voiceless postalveolar affricate")/ and /[d͡ʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_affricate "Voiced postalveolar affricate")/ have become phonemic due to loan words, and /w/ has similarly been re-introduced.

Notes:

1. Proto-Semitic *\*ś* was still pronounced as [[ɬ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_lateral_fricative "Voiceless alveolar lateral fricative")] in Biblical Hebrew, but no letter was available in the Phoenician alphabet, so the letter ש‎ had two pronunciations, representing both /ʃ/ and /ɬ/. Later on, however, /ɬ/ merged with /s/, but the old spelling was largely retained, and the two pronunciations of ש‎ were distinguished graphically in [Tiberian Hebrew](/wiki/Tiberian_Hebrew "Tiberian Hebrew") as שׁ‎ /ʃ/ vs. שׂ‎ /s/ < /ɬ/.
2. Biblical Hebrew as of the 3rd century BCE apparently still distinguished the phonemes *ġ* versus *ʻ* and *ḫ* versus *ḥ*, as witnessed by transcriptions in the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint"). As in the case of /ɬ/, no letters were available to represent these sounds, and existing letters did double duty: ח‎ for /χ/ and /ħ/ and ע‎ for /ʁ/ and /ʕ/. In all of these cases, however, the sounds represented by the same letter eventually merged, leaving no evidence (other than early transcriptions) of the former distinctions.
3. Hebrew and Aramaic underwent [begadkefat](/wiki/Begadkefat "Begadkefat") spirantization at a certain point, whereby the stop sounds /b ɡ d k p t/ were [softened](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") to the corresponding fricatives [v ɣ ð x f θ] (written *ḇ ḡ ḏ ḵ p̄ ṯ*) when occurring after a vowel and not geminated. This change probably happened after the original Old Aramaic phonemes /θ, ð/ disappeared in the 7th century BCE,[[99]](#cite_note-108) and most likely occurred after the loss of Hebrew /χ, ʁ/ c. 200 BCE.[[note 10]](#cite_note-109) It is known to have occurred in Hebrew by the 2nd century.[[100]](#cite_note-110) After a certain point this alternation became contrastive in word-medial and final position (though bearing low [functional load](/wiki/Functional_load "Functional load")), but in word-initial position they remained allophonic.[[101]](#cite_note-bbgd-111) In [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"), the distinction has a higher functional load due to the loss of gemination, although only the three fricatives /v χ f/ are still preserved (the fricative /x/ is pronounced /χ/ in modern Hebrew). (The others are pronounced like the corresponding stops, as Modern Hebrew pronunciation was based on the [Sephardic pronunciation](/wiki/Sephardic_pronunciation "Sephardic pronunciation") which lost the distinction)

## Grammar

Main article: [Modern Hebrew grammar](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew_grammar "Modern Hebrew grammar")

Further information: [History of Hebrew grammar](/wiki/History_of_Hebrew_grammar "History of Hebrew grammar")

Hebrew grammar is partly [analytic](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"), expressing such forms as [dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case"), [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case") and [accusative](/wiki/Accusative_case "Accusative case") using [prepositional](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") particles rather than [grammatical cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"). However, inflection plays a decisive role in the formation of verbs and nouns. For example, nouns have a [construct state](/wiki/Construct_state "Construct state"), called "*smikhut*", to denote the relationship of "belonging to": this is the converse of the [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") of more inflected languages. Words in *smikhut* are often combined with [hyphens](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen"). In modern speech, the use of the construct is sometimes interchangeable with the preposition "*shel*", meaning "of". There are many cases, however, where older declined forms are retained (especially in idiomatic expressions and the like), and "person"-[enclitics](/wiki/Enclitic "Enclitic") are widely used to "decline" prepositions.

### Morphology

Like all Semitic languages, the Hebrew language exhibits a pattern of stems consisting typically of "[triliteral](/wiki/Triliteral "Triliteral")", or 3-consonant [consonantal roots](/wiki/Triliteral "Triliteral"), from which nouns, adjectives, and verbs are formed in various ways: e.g. by inserting vowels, doubling consonants, lengthening vowels and/or adding prefixes, suffixes or [infixes](/wiki/Infix "Infix"). 4-consonant roots also exist and became more frequent in the modern language due to a process of coining verbs from nouns that are themselves constructed from 3-consonant verbs. Some triliteral roots lose one of their consonants in most forms and are called "Nakhim" (Resting).

Hebrew uses a number of [one-letter prefixes](/wiki/Prefixes_in_Hebrew "Prefixes in Hebrew") that are added to words for various purposes. These are called inseparable prepositions or "Letters of Use" (Hebrew: אותיות השימוש, romanized: *Otiyot HaShimush*). Such items include: the definite [article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") *ha-* (/ha/) (= "the"); [prepositions](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") *be-* (/be/) (= "in"), *le-* (/le/) (= "to"; a shortened version of the preposition *el*), *mi-* (/mi/) (= "from"; a shortened version of the preposition *min*); [conjunctions](/wiki/Grammatical_conjunction "Grammatical conjunction") *ve-* (/ve/) (= "and"), *she-* (/ʃe/) (= "that"; a shortened version of the Biblical conjunction *asher*), *ke-* (/ke/) (= "as", "like"; a shortened version of the conjunction *kmo*).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/%D7%A2%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%AA_handwritten_form.jpg/250px-%D7%A2%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%AA_handwritten_form.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%A2%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%AA_handwritten_form.jpg)

The Hebrew word for "Hebrew" (עברית) in its [cursive form](/wiki/Cursive_Hebrew "Cursive Hebrew")

The vowel accompanying each of these letters may differ from those listed above, depending on the first letter or vowel following it. The rules governing these changes are hardly observed in colloquial speech as most speakers tend to employ the regular form. However, they may be heard in more formal circumstances. For example, if a preposition is put before a word that begins with a moving [Shva](/wiki/Shva "Shva"), then the preposition takes the vowel /i/ (and the initial consonant may be weakened): colloquial *be-kfar* (= "in a village") corresponds to the more formal *bi-khfar*.

The definite article may be inserted between a preposition or a conjunction and the word it refers to, creating composite words like *mé-ha-kfar* (= "from the village"). The latter also demonstrates the change in the vowel of *mi-*. With *be*, *le* and *ke*, the definite article is assimilated into the prefix, which then becomes *ba*, *la* or *ka*. Thus \**be-ha-matos* becomes *ba-matos* (= "in the plane"). This does not happen to *mé* (the form of "min" or "mi-" used before the letter "he"), therefore *mé-ha-matos* is a valid form, which means "from the airplane".

:   *\* indicates that the given example is grammatically [non-standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language")*.

### Syntax

Like most other languages, the vocabulary of the Hebrew language is divided into verbs, nouns, adjectives and so on, and its sentence structure can be analyzed by terms like object, subject and so on.

* Though early [Biblical Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") had a [VSO](/wiki/Verb%E2%80%93subject%E2%80%93object_word_order "Verb–subject–object word order") ordering, this gradually transitioned to a subject-verb-object ordering. Many Hebrew sentences have several correct orders of words.
* In Hebrew, there is no [indefinite article](/wiki/Indefinite_article "Indefinite article").
* Hebrew sentences do not have to include verbs; the [copula](/wiki/Copula_(linguistics) "Copula (linguistics)") in the [present tense](/wiki/Present_tense "Present tense") is omitted. For example, the sentence "I am here" (אני פה **ani po**) has only two words; one for I (אני) and one for here (פה). In the sentence "I am that person" (אני הוא האדם הזה **ani hu ha'adam ha'ze**), the word for "am" corresponds to the word for "he" (הוא). However, this is usually omitted. Thus, the sentence (אני האדם הזה) is more often used and means the same thing.
* Negative and interrogative sentences have the same order as the regular declarative one. A question that has a yes/no answer begins with "האם" (*ha'im*, an interrogative form of 'if'), but it is largely omitted in informal speech.
* In Hebrew there is a specific preposition (את **et**) for direct objects that would not have a preposition marker in English. The English phrase "he ate the cake" would in Hebrew be הוא אכל את העוגה **hu akhal et ha'ugah** (literally, "He ate את the cake"). The word את, however, can be omitted, making הוא אכל העוגה **hu akhal ha'ugah** ("He ate the cake"). Former Israeli Prime Minister [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") was convinced that את should never be used as it elongates the sentence without adding meaning.
* In spoken Hebrew ‏את ה־‏ *et ha-* is also often contracted to ‏תַ׳־‏ *ta-*, e.g. ת׳אנשים *ta-anashim* instead of את האנשים *et ha-anashim* (the [geresh](/wiki/Geresh "Geresh") indicates non-standard use). This phenomenon has also been found by researchers in the [Bar Kokhba documents](/wiki/Cave_of_Letters#Bar-Kokhba_letters "Cave of Letters"): מעיד אני עלי **תשמים**… שאני נותן **תכבלים** ברגליכם, writing תללו instead of את הללו, as well as תדקל and so on.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Writing system

Main articles: [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet") and [Hebrew braille](/wiki/Hebrew_braille "Hebrew braille")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Frank-ruehl.png/250px-Frank-ruehl.png)](/wiki/File:Frank-ruehl.png)

[Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet")

Users of the language write Modern Hebrew from [right to left](/wiki/Right-to-left "Right-to-left") using the [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet") – an "impure" [abjad](/wiki/Abjad "Abjad"), or consonant-only script, of 22 letters. The ancient [Paleo-Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Paleo-Hebrew_alphabet "Paleo-Hebrew alphabet") resembles those used for [Canaanite](/wiki/Canaanite_language "Canaanite language") and [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_language "Phoenician language").[[102]](#cite_note-112)[[103]](#cite_note-113) Modern scripts derive from the "square" letter form, known as *Ashurit* (Assyrian), which developed from the [Aramaic script](/wiki/Aramaic_script "Aramaic script"). A [cursive Hebrew](/wiki/Cursive_Hebrew "Cursive Hebrew") script is used in handwriting: the letters tend to appear more circular in form when written in cursive, and sometimes vary markedly from their printed equivalents. The medieval version of the cursive script forms the basis of another style, known as [Rashi script](/wiki/Rashi_script "Rashi script"). When necessary, vowels are indicated by [diacritic](/wiki/Hebrew_diacritics "Hebrew diacritics") marks above or below the letter representing the syllabic onset, or by use of *[matres lectionis](/wiki/Matres_lectionis "Matres lectionis")*, which are consonantal letters used as vowels. Further diacritics may serve to indicate variations in the pronunciation of the consonants (e.g. *bet*/*vet*, *shin*/*sin*); and, in some contexts, to indicate the punctuation, accentuation and musical rendition of Biblical texts (see [Hebrew cantillation](/wiki/Hebrew_cantillation "Hebrew cantillation")).

Hebrew has always been used as the language of prayer and study, and the following pronunciation systems are found.

[Ashkenazi Hebrew](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Hebrew "Ashkenazi Hebrew"), originating in Central and Eastern Europe, is still widely used in Ashkenazi Jewish religious services and studies in Israel and abroad, particularly in the [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi "Haredi") and other [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") communities. It was influenced by [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") pronunciation.

[Sephardi Hebrew](/wiki/Sephardi_Hebrew "Sephardi Hebrew") is the traditional pronunciation of the [Spanish and Portuguese Jews](/wiki/Spanish_and_Portuguese_Jews "Spanish and Portuguese Jews") and [Sephardi Jews](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") in the countries of the former [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), with the exception of [Yemenite Hebrew](/wiki/Yemenite_Hebrew "Yemenite Hebrew"). This pronunciation, in the form used by the Jerusalem Sephardic community, is the basis of the [Hebrew phonology](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew_phonology "Modern Hebrew phonology") of Israeli native speakers. It was influenced by [Ladino](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") pronunciation.

[Mizrahi (Oriental) Hebrew](/wiki/Mizrahi_Hebrew "Mizrahi Hebrew") is actually a collection of dialects spoken liturgically by Jews in various parts of the [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") and [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") world. It was derived from the old [Arabic language](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), and in some cases influenced by Sephardi Hebrew. [Yemenite Hebrew](/wiki/Yemenite_Hebrew "Yemenite Hebrew") or *Temanit* differs from other Mizrahi dialects by having a radically different vowel system, and distinguishing between different diacritically marked consonants that are pronounced identically in other dialects (for example gimel and "ghimel".)

These pronunciations are still used in synagogue ritual and religious study in Israel and elsewhere, mostly by people who are not native speakers of Hebrew. However, some traditionalist Israelis use liturgical pronunciations in prayer.

Many synagogues in the diaspora, even though Ashkenazi by rite and by ethnic composition, have adopted the "Sephardic" pronunciation in deference to Israeli Hebrew. However, in many British and American schools and synagogues, this pronunciation retains several elements of its Ashkenazi substrate, especially the distinction between [tsere](/wiki/Tzere "Tzere") and [segol](/wiki/Segol "Segol").
